title: GCS: Eye Opening
subtitle: Assess eye opening using the correct clinical sequence.

cards.frontPrompt: What is the Glasgow Coma Scale used for?
backPoints: The Glasgow Coma Scale provides a standardised assessment of a patient's level of consciousness.
It allows neurological findings to be communicated consistently.
Repeated assessments help identify deterioration or improvement.
The GCS assesses Eye Opening, Verbal Response and Motor Response.
cards.memoryLine: A consistent assessment allows reliable neurological trending.

cards.frontPrompt: How should Eye Opening be assessed?
backPoints: First observe whether the patient's eyes are already open.
If their eyes are closed, speak to them.
Only use a painful stimulus if there is no response to voice.
Record the best response demonstrated during a valid assessment.

cards.frontPrompt: Why must voice be tested before pain?
backPoints: A patient who opens their eyes to voice scores E3.
The same patient may also open their eyes when pain is applied.
If pain is used first, you cannot know whether they would have responded to voice.
Recording E2 after skipping voice could make the patient's GCS appear lower than it really is.
cards.memoryLine: Skipping voice can produce an invalidly low Eye Opening score.

assessment.instructions: Assess the patient using the correct sequence, then select the Eye Opening score.

painBeforeVoice.heading: Assessment sequence invalid
painBeforeVoice.message: Pain was applied before voice. The patient may also have opened their eyes to voice, so you cannot assign a valid Eye Opening score. Restart and assess in the correct order: Observe → Voice → Pain.

correct.heading: Correct assessment
correct.message: Correct. You used the appropriate assessment sequence and selected the correct Eye Opening score.

incorrectScore.heading: Reconsider the response
incorrectScore.message: The selected score does not match the response demonstrated during this assessment.
